core_concept.principle: Electron configuration describes how electrons are distributed among the energy levels and orbitals of an atom, determining its chemical properties.
core_concept.explanation: Electrons occupy specific energy levels and sublevels in a predictable pattern based on their energy. This arrangement, called electron configuration, explains why elements in the same group have similar chemical properties and helps predict how atoms will bond and react.

image.alt: Diagram showing electron configuration notation with energy levels and orbitals
image.caption: Electron configuration shows how electrons are arranged in an atom's energy levels.

sections.introduction: The arrangement of electrons in an atom determines how it will interact with other atoms. Understanding electron configuration is essential for predicting chemical bonding, reactivity, and the properties of elements.

introduction_image.alt: Illustration showing electrons involved in chemical bonding
introduction_image.caption: Electron arrangement determines chemical behavior and bonding.

key_concept.definition: Electron configuration is the distribution of electrons among the orbitals of an atom, written using a standardized notation that indicates energy levels, sublevels, and number of electrons.
key_concept.context: Electron configuration follows specific rules based on quantum mechanics. It explains the periodic table's structure and why elements in the same group behave similarly.

image.alt: Example electron configuration notation for several elements
image.caption: Standard notation shows energy level, sublevel, and electron count.

sections.introduction: Electrons occupy principal energy levels (shells) that contain sublevels (subshells). Each sublevel contains orbitals where electrons are most likely to be found.

classifications.value: Main energy shells numbered n = 1, 2, 3, etc., with higher numbers farther from nucleus
characteristics: Designated by principal quantum number n
Higher n values have higher energy
Electrons fill lower energy levels first
classifications.behavior: Electrons occupy the lowest available energy level first, then fill higher levels as needed.

examples.explanation: Closest to nucleus, lowest energy, holds maximum 2 electrons. Only contains s sublevel.

image.alt: First energy level with 2 electrons
image.caption: The first energy level holds a maximum of 2 electrons.

examples.explanation: Second shell from nucleus, holds maximum 8 electrons. Contains s and p sublevels.

image.alt: Second energy level with 8 electrons
image.caption: The second energy level holds a maximum of 8 electrons.

classifications.value: subdivisions within energy levels that have different shapes and electron capacities
characteristics: s sublevel: 1 orbital, holds 2 electrons, spherical shape
p sublevel: 3 orbitals, holds 6 electrons, dumbbell shape
d sublevel: 5 orbitals, holds 10 electrons, complex shape
f sublevel: 7 orbitals, holds 14 electrons, very complex shape
classifications.behavior: Each energy level contains specific sublevels: n=1 has only s; n=2 has s and p; n=3 has s, p, and d; n=4 has s, p, d, and f.

examples.explanation: Found in all energy levels. Spherical orbital centered on nucleus. Holds 2 electrons maximum.

image.alt: Spherical s orbital diagram
image.caption: s orbitals are spherical and hold 2 electrons.

examples.explanation: Found in energy levels n≥2. Three dumbbell-shaped orbitals oriented along x, y, z axes. Holds 6 electrons maximum.

image.alt: Three p orbitals showing dumbbell shapes
image.caption: p orbitals have dumbbell shapes and hold 6 electrons.

classifications.value: Regions of space where electrons are most likely to be found; each orbital holds maximum 2 electrons
characteristics: Each orbital can hold exactly 2 electrons with opposite spins
Orbitals within a sublevel have equal energy (degenerate)
Different sublevels have different orbital shapes
Electron position is probabilistic, not fixed
classifications.behavior: Electrons fill orbitals according to specific rules that minimize the atom's energy.

examples.explanation: One orbital = 2 electrons maximum. s has 1 orbital (2 e⁻), p has 3 orbitals (6 e⁻), d has 5 orbitals (10 e⁻), f has 7 orbitals (14 e⁻).

image.alt: Diagram showing orbital electron capacity
image.caption: Each orbital holds exactly 2 electrons.

sections.introduction: Three fundamental rules govern how electrons fill orbitals: the Aufbau principle, the Pauli exclusion principle, and Hund's rule.

principles.statement: Electrons fill orbitals starting with the lowest energy level first, then proceed to higher energy levels.
principles.application: Use the diagonal rule or energy level diagram to determine filling order: 1s → 2s → 2p → 3s → 3p → 4s → 3d → 4p → 5s → 4d → 5p → 6s → 4f → 5d → 6p → 7s → 5f → 6d → 7p
principles.analogy: Like filling seats in a theater: people fill the front rows (lowest energy) first before moving to back rows (higher energy).
principles.example: Hydrogen (1 electron): 1s¹; Helium (2 electrons): 1s²; Lithium (3 electrons): 1s² 2s¹

image.alt: Diagonal rule diagram showing orbital filling order
image.caption: The diagonal rule shows the order electrons fill orbitals.

principles.statement: No two electrons in an atom can have the same set of four quantum numbers; each orbital can hold maximum 2 electrons with opposite spins.
principles.application: When writing electron configuration, no orbital can have more than 2 electrons. Represented as ↑↓ in orbital diagrams.
principles.analogy: Like a parking space: only two cars (electrons) can fit, and they must face opposite directions (opposite spins).
principles.example: A 2p orbital can hold 2 electrons: one spin up (↑) and one spin down (↓), written as 2p².

image.alt: Orbital diagram showing opposite electron spins
image.caption: Electrons in the same orbital must have opposite spins.

principles.statement: When filling orbitals of equal energy (degenerate orbitals), electrons occupy empty orbitals singly before pairing up.
principles.application: In p, d, or f sublevels, place one electron in each orbital before adding second electrons. All singly-occupied orbitals have parallel spins.
principles.analogy: Like people on a bus: passengers sit in empty seats first before sharing seats with strangers.
principles.example: Nitrogen (7 electrons): 1s² 2s² 2p³ - each of the three 2p orbitals gets one electron before any pairing occurs.

image.alt: Orbital diagram showing Hund's rule with single electrons
image.caption: Electrons fill empty orbitals singly before pairing.

sections.introduction: Electron configurations are written using a standard notation that indicates the energy level, sublevel, and number of electrons in each sublevel.

process_flow.description: For a neutral atom, the number of electrons equals the atomic number (number of protons).
process_flow.mechanism: Find the element on the periodic table; the atomic number gives the electron count for neutral atoms.
process_flow.result: Total electron count is established for configuration.

image.alt: Periodic table showing atomic number
image.caption: Atomic number equals electron count in neutral atoms.

process_flow.description: Fill orbitals in order of increasing energy using the diagonal rule.
process_flow.mechanism: Start with 1s, then 2s, 2p, 3s, 3p, 4s, 3d, etc., adding electrons until all are placed.
process_flow.result: Electrons are distributed among orbitals correctly.

image.alt: Orbital filling order diagram
image.caption: Follow the energy order when filling orbitals.

process_flow.description: Ensure no orbital has more than 2 electrons; fill degenerate orbitals singly before pairing.
process_flow.mechanism: Check each sublevel: s gets max 2, p gets max 6, d gets max 10, f gets max 14.
process_flow.result: Configuration follows all quantum mechanical rules.

image.alt: Orbital diagram with correct electron placement
image.caption: Apply all three rules for correct configuration.

process_flow.description: Write the configuration using standard notation: energy level + sublevel + superscript electron count.
process_flow.result: Complete electron configuration is written correctly.

image.alt: Complete electron configuration written out
image.caption: Standard notation shows level, sublevel, and electron count.

sections.introduction: Practice writing electron configurations for various elements to master the notation and rules.

worked_examples.title: Electron Configuration of Carbon
worked_examples.problem: Write the electron configuration for carbon (atomic number 6).

step_by_step.explanation: Carbon has atomic number 6, so a neutral carbon atom has 6 electrons.
Fill orbitals in Aufbau order: 1s first (holds 2), then 2s (holds 2), then 2p (holds remaining).
Continue filling: 2s gets 2 electrons, then 2p gets the remaining 2 electrons.
Verify: 2 + 2 + 2 = 6 electrons total. Configuration is complete.

worked_examples.concept_applied: Aufbau principle determines filling order; total electrons equal atomic number for neutral atoms.

image.alt: Carbon electron configuration with orbital diagram
image.caption: Carbon has 4 valence electrons in the second energy level.

worked_examples.title: Electron Configuration of Sodium
worked_examples.problem: Write the electron configuration for sodium (atomic number 11).

step_by_step.explanation: Sodium has atomic number 11, so a neutral sodium atom has 11 electrons.
Fill orbitals in order: 1s² (2), 2s² (4 total), 2p⁶ (10 total).
The remaining 1 electron goes into the next orbital: 3s¹.
Verify: 2 + 2 + 6 + 1 = 11 electrons. The single 3s electron is the valence electron.

worked_examples.concept_applied: The outermost electron (3s¹) determines sodium's chemical properties and +1 ion formation.

image.alt: Sodium electron configuration showing valence electron
image.caption: Sodium has 1 valence electron in the third energy level.

worked_examples.title: Electron Configuration of Chlorine
worked_examples.problem: Write the electron configuration for chlorine (atomic number 17).

step_by_step.explanation: Chlorine has atomic number 17, so a neutral chlorine atom has 17 electrons.
Remaining 5 electrons go into 3p sublevel (can hold 6).
Verify: 2 + 2 + 6 + 2 + 5 = 17 electrons. Seven valence electrons (3s² 3p⁵) explain chlorine's -1 ion tendency.

worked_examples.concept_applied: Seven valence electrons mean chlorine needs one more electron to complete its outer shell, explaining its high reactivity.

image.alt: Chlorine electron configuration with valence electrons highlighted
image.caption: Chlorine has 7 valence electrons, needing 1 more for stability.

sections.introduction: For larger atoms, writing full electron configurations becomes lengthy. Noble gas notation provides a shorthand method using the previous noble gas as a reference.

categories.description: Writing out all energy levels and sublevels from 1s to the outermost electrons.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Complete notation shows every electron but becomes cumbersome for heavy elements. Useful for learning and small atoms.

image.alt: Full electron configuration written out
image.caption: Full configuration shows every electron.

categories.description: Using the symbol of the previous noble gas in brackets to represent core electrons, then writing only valence electrons.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Find the noble gas that comes before the element on the periodic table. Replace all electrons up to that noble gas with its symbol in brackets, then write remaining electrons.

examples.explanation: Previous noble gas is argon (18 electrons). Potassium is [Ar] 4s¹ - much shorter than full configuration.

image.alt: Noble gas shorthand notation example
image.caption: Noble gas notation simplifies writing configurations.

examples.explanation: Previous noble gas is neon (10 electrons). Chlorine is [Ne] 3s² 3p⁵.

image.alt: Chlorine noble gas notation
image.caption: Only valence electrons are written after the noble gas.

sections.introduction: Valence electrons are the electrons in the outermost energy level. They determine an element's chemical properties and bonding behavior.

process_types.function: Valence electrons are those in the highest principal energy level (highest n value)
goals: Look at the highest energy level number in the configuration
Count all electrons in that level (s and p for main group elements)
For transition metals, include d electrons in some cases
Valence electrons determine group number for main group elements

primary_example.explanation: Sodium: 1s² 2s² 2p⁶ 3s¹. Highest level is n=3, which has 1 electron (3s¹). Sodium has 1 valence electron, matching Group 1.

image.alt: Sodium configuration with valence electron highlighted
image.caption: The outermost electron is the valence electron.

examples.explanation: Oxygen: 1s² 2s² 2p⁴. Highest level n=2 has 2 + 4 = 6 valence electrons, matching Group 16.
Argon: 1s² 2s² 2p⁶ 3s² 3p⁶. Highest level n=3 has 2 + 6 = 8 valence electrons (full octet).

process_types.function: Elements react to achieve stable electron configurations, usually 8 valence electrons (octet rule)
goals: Metals lose valence electrons to form positive ions
Nonmetals gain electrons to form negative ions
Noble gases already have 8 valence electrons (stable)
Number of valence electrons predicts ion charge

primary_example.explanation: Sodium (1 valence electron) loses 1 electron to form Na⁺, achieving the stable configuration of neon. This explains sodium's +1 charge.

image.alt: Sodium atom losing electron to become ion
image.caption: Losing valence electron creates stable ion.

examples.explanation: Chlorine (7 valence electrons) gains 1 electron to form Cl⁻, achieving argon's stable configuration.
Magnesium (2 valence electrons) loses 2 electrons to form Mg²⁺, achieving neon's stable configuration.

process_types.function: Elements in the same group have the same number of valence electrons, explaining similar chemical properties
goals: Group 18: 8 valence electrons (noble gases, except He)

primary_example.explanation: Lithium [He] 2s¹, Sodium [Ne] 3s¹, Potassium [Ar] 4s¹ - all have 1 valence electron, explaining why they all form +1 ions and react similarly with water.

image.alt: Group 1 elements showing same valence configuration
image.caption: Same group = same valence electrons = similar properties.

examples.explanation: Fluorine, chlorine, bromine all have 7 valence electrons, all form -1 ions, all react vigorously with metals.
All have 8 valence electrons (full outer shell), making them chemically inert and unreactive.

sections.relationship: Electron configuration determines valence electrons, which determine chemical properties and bonding behavior. This explains the periodic table's organization and element reactivity patterns.

sections.introduction: Some elements have electron configurations that don't follow the expected Aufbau order due to extra stability of half-filled or fully-filled sublevels.

principles.statement: Chromium (Cr, atomic number 24) has configuration [Ar] 4s¹ 3d⁵ instead of expected [Ar] 4s² 3d⁴.
principles.application: A half-filled d sublevel (d⁵) is more stable than a partially filled one. One electron moves from 4s to 3d to achieve this stability.
principles.analogy: Like preferring evenly distributed teams: having 5 players on each of two teams (half-filled) is more stable than 6 and 4.

image.alt: Chromium electron configuration showing exception
image.caption: Chromium moves one electron to achieve half-filled d sublevel.

principles.statement: Copper (Cu, atomic number 29) has configuration [Ar] 4s¹ 3d¹⁰ instead of expected [Ar] 4s² 3d⁹.
principles.application: A fully-filled d sublevel (d¹⁰) is more stable than a partially filled one. One electron moves from 4s to 3d to achieve this stability.
principles.analogy: Like completing a collection: having all 10 items (full set) is more satisfying than having 9.

image.alt: Copper electron configuration showing exception
image.caption: Copper moves one electron to achieve fully-filled d sublevel.

summary: Electron configuration describes how electrons are distributed among an atom's orbitals using standard notation (e.g., 1s² 2s² 2p⁶). Three rules govern electron filling: Aufbau principle (fill lowest energy first), Pauli exclusion principle (max 2 electrons per orbital with opposite spins), and Hund's rule (fill degenerate orbitals singly before pairing). Energy levels (n=1, 2, 3...) contain sublevels (s, p, d, f) which contain orbitals (s=1, p=3, d=5, f=7). Each orbital holds 2 electrons maximum. Valence electrons are those in the outermost energy level and determine chemical properties and bonding behavior. Elements in the same group have the same number of valence electrons, explaining similar chemical properties. Noble gas notation uses the previous noble gas symbol in brackets to abbreviate configurations. Some elements (Cr, Cu) have exceptions where electrons shift to achieve half-filled or fully-filled sublevel stability. Understanding electron configuration explains the periodic table's structure, predicts ion formation, and enables understanding of chemical bonding.
